coredata.dc:title: Relevant photovoltaic effect in N-doped CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Krishan
Kumar, Anuj
Devi, Sarita
Tyagi, Sachin
Kaur, Davinder
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel kind of solar cell based on the N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure has been fabricated using a two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 ultrathin layer and zero-dimensional (0D) Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The combination of N-CQDs and MoS2 offers good optical and electrical characteristics. The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the quantum dimensional heterostructure have been recorded in the dark and under the illumination of solar radiations. A significant current rectification ratio has been observed in dark I–V characteristics. Furthermore, a remarkable shift of −13.49 mA in the I–V curve is observed on the exposure to solar radiation. The fabricated quantum dimensional heterostructure exhibits a significant power conversion efficiency (η) of 4.06% and an open-circuit voltage of 0.83 V. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra support the recorded photovoltaic performance of the heterostructure. The aforementioned results could be ascribed to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs at the junction contact. The N-CQDs serve as an electron blocking layer, preventing additional carrier recombination at the metal electrode. Our findings show that N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure has a great potential in futuristic low-cost, non-toxic, and highly efficient solar cells for next-generation energy harvesting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide
Carbon quantum dots
N-CQDs
0D/2D heterostructure
Solar cells
and photovoltaic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Deposition of ultrathin MoS2 layer via sputtering
Deposition of N-CQDs on MoS2/ITO coated glass substrate

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by the Department of Science & Technology (DST), India, under the Nano Mission with reference no. SR/NM/NT-1065/2015 (G), Defence Research Development Organization (DRDO) under EP & IPR 2018 with reference No. ERIP/ER/99011650/M/01/1739 (G) and Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) with reference No. CRG/2020/005265 . Krishan Kumar gratefully thanks to University Grant Commission ( UGC ), India for awarding a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis process of colloidal N-CQDs solution and fabrication of N-CQDs layer via electrophoretic deposition.
(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of MoS2 N-CQDs and heterostructure N-CQDs/MoS2 fabricated on ITO substrate, and (b) FTIR transmittance spectra of synthesized N-CQDs.

AFM images of (a) MoS2 ultrathin layer deposited on ITO substrate, and (b) CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure fabricated over ITO substrate. FE-SEM images of (c) MoS2 layer deposited on ITO substrate, and (d) N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure fabricated on ITO substrate. (e) TEM images of N-CQDs drop casted on a copper grid, inset of (e) shows TEM images at higher resolutions with size measurement of N-CQDs, and (f) SAED pattern of N-CQDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Mo 3d orbital state, (b) S 2p orbital state in MoS2, (c) C 1s orbital state, and (d) N 1s orbital state in N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Visible absorbance spectra of N-CQDs, (b) Tauc's plot for corresponding UV–vis absorbance spectra, (Inset b) N-CQDs band structure under UV-illumination, (c) PL emission spectra of N-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths, (d) PL emission spectra with 340 nm excitation wavelength and corresponding absorbance spectra. Optical images synthesized colloidal N-CQDs solution in (e) daylight, (f) under UV illumination, and (g) N-CQDs deposited over ITO coated glass substrate.

(a) Schematic diagram of N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) based complete quantum dimensional heterostructure photovoltaic device, (b) dark I–V characteristics of as-fabricated heterostructure device, (inset of b) dark I–V characteristics of the MoS2 layer fabricated on ITO substrate, (c) semilogarithmic plot of dark I–V characteristics and (d) The I–V characteristics of as-fabricated heterostructure device in the dark and under the illumination of solar radiations. (e) The replot of I–V curve under solar illumination in voltage ranges 0 to Voc.

(a) Schematic energy band diagram of N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) based quantum dimensional heterostructure under solar illumination. (b) External quantum efficiency spectrum of N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure based solar cell.
EIS curve (Nyquist Plot) of N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2/D) quantum dimensional heterostructure.
Photovoltaic parameters for various quantum dimensional heterostructures based solar cells.

head.ce:title: Relevant photovoltaic effect in N-doped CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Nanomaterials Research Lab, Department of Physics and Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee, 247667, Uttarakhand, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Centre for Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Nanomaterials Research Lab, Department of Physics and Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee-247667, Uttarakhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Techniques Division, CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Techniques Division, CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel kind of solar cell based on the N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure has been fabricated using a two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 ultrathin layer and zero-dimensional (0D) Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The combination of N-CQDs and MoS2 offers good optical and electrical characteristics. The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the quantum dimensional heterostructure have been recorded in the dark and under the illumination of solar radiations. A significant current rectification ratio has been observed in dark I–V characteristics. Furthermore, a remarkable shift of −13.49 mA in the I–V curve is observed on the exposure to solar radiation. The fabricated quantum dimensional heterostructure exhibits a significant power conversion efficiency (η) of 4.06% and an open-circuit voltage of 0.83 V. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra support the recorded photovoltaic performance of the heterostructure. The aforementioned results could be ascribed to the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs at the junction contact. The N-CQDs serve as an electron blocking layer, preventing additional carrier recombination at the metal electrode. Our findings show that N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure has a great potential in futuristic low-cost, non-toxic, and highly efficient solar cells for next-generation energy harvesting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy harvesting is one of the most efficient ways to meet the ever-increasing need for renewable energy. The majority of existing photovoltaic (PV) technology comprises crystalline silicon (c-Si) p-n homojunction based solar cells, owing to their high efficiency and extended lifespan [1,2]. Although the vast financial expense of utilizing the highly purified silicon wafers and high-temperature manufacturing process are the major problems for the current solar cell industry [1,3]. Interest in the novel approach of solar cell technologies has increased with the emergence of quantum dots (QDs) such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) cadmium selenide (CdSe),cadmium sulphide (CdTe), and lead sulphide (PbS) QDs, based solar cells owing to their size-dependent tunable optical bandgap and efficient multiple exciton generation that can have the potential to exceed the Shockley- Queisser (S-Q) limit [4–6]. The integration of two-dimensional (2D) semiconducting materials with zero-dimensional (0D) semiconducting QDs can be an excellent choice of heterojunctions to obtain lightweight and cost-effective photovoltaic devices with better performance because QDs can generate multiple excitons on the incident of a single photon falling on it [7].

2D semiconducting transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have recently received much attention of researchers due to their exceptional properties in the field of flexible and wearable electronics [8,9]. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is the most striking and auspicious material within the TMDs family due to its inimitable electrical band structure with unique indirect to direct bandgap transitions which depend on the number of constituent layers [10]. 2-D layered MoS2 has a hexagonal lattice configuration consisting of a layer of molybdenum atoms sandwiched between two layers of sulfur atoms in the S–Mo–S configuration [11]. Also, MoS2 possesses a strong excitonic effect, tunable optical properties, high breakdown voltage, superior electron mobility (480 cm2/Vs), mechanical flexibility, decent current on/off ratio (⁓107), a well-suitable surface to volume ratio, and non-toxic nature [12,13].

Fluorescent CQDs have gained a lot of attention over the last few years because of their peculiar optical and electronic properties [14]. CQDs are 0D quasi-spherical nanostructured carbon allotropes having amorphous to the crystalline core with sp2 (turbostratic carbon) hybridization fused by sp3 (diamond type) hybridization [15]. Since their discovery, CQDs have been the focus of study because of their peculiar properties such as outstanding solubility, simplicity of functionalization, chemical stability, emerging high photoluminescence, less toxic, and resistance to photobleaching compared to their 1D equivalents [16–18]. Such outstanding properties make them useful in various applications such as optoelectronics, bioimaging, fluorescent sensors, biosensing, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), and solar cells [19–21]. Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) exhibit excellent optical properties with an emerging blue shift in fluorescence spectra which extend the scope of CQDs in various optoelectronic applications [22,23]. Furthermore, pristine CQDs or doped CQDs have been fabricated with semiconducting 2D materials such as MoS2 and metal oxides for practical applications such as solar cells, photodetectors, sensors, and supercapacitors, etc. [24–26]. Although N-CQDs possess high carrier mobility, size-dependent tunable bandgap, multiple exciton generation, and a dual-functional electron acceptor-donor nature, the reported research on N-CQDs-based photovoltaic devices is relatively limited [27–30]. Previous studies on 2D–0D hybrid photovoltaic devices have focused on silicon substrates and heavy metal quantum dots with a restricted spectral range. Heavy metal-based quantum dots are costly and hazardous to human health and the atmosphere [33]. It would be more beneficial to use such non-hazardous materials based devices for a long-term future target.

This paper demonstrate an N-CQDs layer deposited on pre-deposited MoS2 on indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass substrate. Here MoS2 layer was coated using DC magnetron sputtering on ITO coated glass substrate, while the ultrathin CQDs layer was fabricated on MoS2/ITO coated glass via electrophoretic deposition. In most of the reports, researchers used CVD techniques for the deposition of both MoS2 and quantum dots layers which are toxic and harmful [33]. Both sputtering and electrophoretic deposition techniques offer many advantages over other deposition techniques like large-scale uniformity, better controllability of deposition parameters, non-toxic and eco-friendly [33,34].

Furthermore, N-CQDs and MoS2 form a p-n like heterojunction at the junction interface, creating a valuable depletion electric field essential for photogenerated exciton separation to electron-hole pairs for carrier transportation. The systematic analysis of the structural, optical, electric and photovoltaic properties of N-CQDs/MoS2/ITO heterostructure based devices have been performed, and their photovoltaic cell parameters are well explained. Fabrication of such a photovoltaic device on a glass substrate would be the best choice in the direction of a cost-effective and efficient photovoltaic device. The integration of MoS2 and N-doped CQDs could significantly lower the cost of multifunctional photovoltaic devices. Also, it could be in the direction of a new prosperous scientific era of photovoltaic devices based on 0D-2D quantum dimensional heterostructure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deposition of ultrathin MoS2 layer via sputtering
ce:section.ce:para: DC Magnetron Sputtering was used for the fabrication of the ultrathin MoS2 layer. The MoS2 thin films were fabricated on ITO-coated glass substrate with DC power, substrate temperature, argon gas pressure, and deposition time at 40 W, 300 °C, 8 mTorr, and 60 s. With these deposition parameters the reported deposition rate of MoS2 is ⁓ 20 nm/min [13]. As a result, the deposited MoS2 ultrathin layer had a thickness of 20 nm. To understand the deposition process a schematic diagram is shown in the supplementary file (Fig. S1).

The synthesis of N-CQDs was performed chemically by heating a precursor containing both nitrogen and carbon. Initially, 0.20 g aminobenzenedicarboxylic (ABDC) acid was added in 20 ml deionized (DI) water and mixed properly. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 hrs. After then the solution was cooled at room temperature. Then ethanol was added to achieve the final volume of 22 ml and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was stored at room temperature for further process. The 6 ml of the reaction supernatant was dialyzed in an activated dialysis membrane (MWCO) against 300 ml DI water for 150 min. The schematic synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 (a–d). The dialysate contains a required N-CQDs concentration for further characterization and electrophoretic deposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deposition of N-CQDs on MoS2/ITO coated glass substrate
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a MoS2 deposited ITO coated glass substrate and ultrasonically cleaned ITO coated glass substrate of dimension 1 × 1 cm2 were fixed on negative and positive electrodes respectively and dipped properly in an electrolyte solution with a separation of 0.8 mm. The colloidal solution of N-CQDs in deionized (DI) water was taken as the electrolyte, and a potential of 1.5 V was applied between both electrodes via some external source for 30 min. After this, N-CQDs were deposited on both electrodes uniformly, producing a thicker and dense layer on the negative electrode experimental setup for the electrophoretic deposition process, as shown in Fig. 1(e). Finally, N-CQDs deposited on MoS2/ITO coated glass substrates were cleaned with DI water, dried at room temperature, and kept for further characterizations.
A fingers-like pattern of Ag was fabricated for top electrode contact on N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure using DC sputtering.

The sputtering target (with 99.99% purity) of MoS2 was purchased from ACI alloys. Aminobenzenedicarboxylic (ABDC) acid for N-CQDs synthesis was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The X-ray diffraction pattern of the ultrathin MoS2 layer was recorded using a Rigaku Smartlab X-ray diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα wavelength. AFM images were obtained to analyze the surface topography of MoS2 thin films using atomic force microscopy (AFM, NT-MDT NTEGRA model). The surface morphology of MoS2 and N-CQDs thin films was examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Gemini, Carl Zeiss) and tunneling electron microscope (TEM) (TECNAI G2, FEI, USA). X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Perkin Elmer model 1257, was used to analyze the chemical structure of MoS2 thin films in MoS2/ITO and N-CQDs/MoS2/ITO heterojunctions. The optical absorbance properties of an ultrathin MoS2 layer and N-CQDs were tested using a Lab India UV-3200 spectrophotometer with a 10-mm path length and incident wavelengths varying from 300 to 800 nm. At excitonic wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 360 nm, the fluorescent properties of as-synthesized N-CQDs were studied using a Varian Carry - 5000 photoluminescence spectrophotometer with a 10 mm quartz cuvette. Current-Voltage (I–V) measurements were taken using the semiconductor characterization setup (SCS - 4200). Solar cells characteristics of fabricated 0D-2D heterostructure device were investigated under AM 1.5G 1 Sun solar illumination using PECCELL (PEC-S20) portable solar simulator with Keithley 2450 sourcemeter and action spectrum measurement system. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was recorded using an autolab potentiostat system.

Fig. 2 (a) depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MoS2/ITO, N-CQDs/ITO and heterostructure N-CQDs/MoS2/ITO plotted with offset y-axis. The XRD pattern of N-CQDs/ITO has a diffraction hump at 23.55°, corresponding to the N-CQDs' (002) reflection plane. The hexagonal phase of the MoS2 thin film is revealed by the XRD pattern of MoS2/ITO, which shows (002) and (103) plane reflections at 13.07° and 38.06°, respectively (JCPDS ICDD no. 00-002-1133). The crystallite parameters calculated for fabricated ultrathin MoS2 layer are shown in Table S2 (supplementary file). The XRD pattern of the N-CQDs/MoS2/ITO heterostructure shows (002) and (103) reflections of MoS2, as well as a (002) oriented reflection plane of the N-CQDs phase, indicating nanostructured heterojunction formation (top curve, Fig. 2(a)). The presence of additional peaks in the XRD pattern is due to corresponding reflection planes of ITO-coated glass substrate.

Fig. 2(b) shows the FTIR transmittance spectra of synthesized N-CQDs. A broad peak centered at 3445 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H (Carboxylic acid) and N–H (amine groups) bonds [35]. The FTIR transmittance peaks centered at 1117 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 shows the presence of graphitic carbon's C–C bonds (sp3 hybridized) and CC (sp3 hybridized) bonds, respectively. A wide dip in FTIR spectra at 2200 to 2400 cm−1 reflects the stretching of CN bonds (sp1 hybridized), which can be attributed to the doping of nitrogen in CQDs [30]. FTIR transmittance peak at 2920 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C–H and C–N bonds (amide) of N-CQDs [30]. FITR transmittance spectra confirms the formation of N-doped CQDs.

Fig. 3 (a and b) compares the AFM images of deposited ultrathin MoS2 layer and N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure. The AFM images of the heterostructure show that the MoS2 thin film is uniform and free from pinholes with an average roughness of 2.6 nm. After the deposition of N-CQDs, the average roughness of thin-film increases 2.6 nm–3.55 nm as QDs fill the surface uniformly, clearly visible on AFM images. Moreover, deposited N-CQDs form a closely packed layer on the surface of the MoS2 layer, which is clearly depicted by the roughness parameters determined from AFM images. The surface morphologies of the bare MoS2 ultrathin layer and N-CQDs/MoS2 heterostructure fabricated on ITO coated glass substrates are shown in FE-SEM images (Fig. 3(c and d)). When N-CQDs are deposited on the MoS2 layer, the sharp nanoworms structured surface morphology of the MoS2 layer transforms into distorted nanoworms structure. TEM images with higher resolution show as-synthesized N-CQDs nearly uniform spherical shaped nanocrystalline morphology and formed with a good yield (Fig. 3(e)). Inset (1) of Fig. 3(e) shows the statistical analysis of measured diameters of N-CQDs detected in TEM images with the number of counts of dots. The average size calculated from the Gaussian fitting of the number of counts was found to be 6.18 nm. The inset (2) of Fig. 3(e) depicts the size distribution of synthesized N-CQDs; labeling of size scale on formed dots confirms all N-CQDs formed with size ranges 4.6–7 nm. Fig. 3 (f) shows the selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of as-synthesized N-CQDs. The interplanar spacing for (002) peak calculated from the SAED pattern was 4.51 Å.

The ex-situ X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was analyzed to further study the surface chemistry and bonding properties of the fabricated N-CQDs/MoS2 quantum dimensional heterostructure. The high-resolution spectra of the Mo 3d orbital state reveal the triplet peaks with binding energies of 229.95 eV, 233.09 eV, and 236.24 eV (Fig. 4 (a)). The high-intensity peaks of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 states confirm the Mo4+ oxidation state in the MoS2 layer. The low-intensity peak at 236.28 eV is responsible for the formation of the Mo6+ state corresponding to MoO3 or MoO4 oxides on the surface [36]. The presence of oxides on the surface may be formed in DI water solution during the electrophoretic deposition process or in an open-air environment. The high-resolution spectra of the S 2p state exhibit two significant peaks with binding energies of 162.62 eV and 163.69 eV are corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 bonding orbital states, respectively. It can be attributed to the S2− in the formation of MoS2 (Fig. 4(b)) [37]. A high-resolution spectrum of C 1s peak contains triplet peaks with binding energies 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 287.7 eV indicate C–C or CC bonding, C–N bonding, CN or CO bonding, respectively (Fig. 4(c)) [38]. The high-resolution spectrum of the N 1s orbital state reveals that the significant 1s peak consists of four peaks with binding energies of 397.65 eV, 398.42 eV, 399.42 eV, and 400.57 eV (Fig. 4(d)) [38,39]. These peaks are attributed to the presence of bonding structures of nitrogen atoms like pyridinic, pyrrolic, amino, and graphitic, respectively.

Fig. 5 (a) depicts the absorption spectra of N-CQDs in UV and visible region, which shows the best intense absorption peak in this region at 224.8 nm and 344.6 nm that can be attributed to the π- π* orbital transition of the sp2 hybridized polyaromatic CC and n–π* orbital transitions of the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs respectively [40,41]. The inset of Fig. 5(a) shows an intense absorption peak formed due to the C–N bonding representing the significant N atom doping in CQDs. This indicates that the colloidal solution contains N-CQDs with uniform size having a diameter ⁓ 5–6 nm (absorption peak at 344.6 nm) [42]. The QDs' size can also be estimated using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement (Supplementary file). The optical energy band gap of N-CQDs obtained from the Tauc's plot is 2.79eV (Fig. 5(b)). N-CQDs' PL emission spectra display the fabricated Quantum dots have high luminescence properties with an emission peak at 443.4 nm. The synthesized N-CQDs also offer good photostability with time. The absorption and emission mechanism has been shown in the schematic energy band diagram of N-CQDs (inset Fig. 5(b)). Both the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) states of energy bands consist of three energy states. These states have been formed due to π, n and π* bonding orbitals of C, N and O atoms [43,44]. The PL emission spectra of N-CQDs with different excited wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 360 nm is shown in Fig. 5(c). The PL emission spectra with an excited wavelength of 340 nm exhibits the high-intensity peak due to the intense absorption peak of synthesized QDs near this wavelength. The optical energy band gap calculated from this emission peak position is 2.80 eV (Fig. 5(d)), similar to that calculated from the Tauc plot. Fig. 5(e–g) show the optical images of as-synthesized N-CQDs in daylight, under UV illumination and the electrophoretically deposited N-CQDs on ITO coated glass substrate under UV illumination, respectively. These optical images support the UV absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of fabricated N-CQDs. The optical energy bandgap of the deposited ultrathin MoS2 layer is 1.37 eV which lies between the reported optical energy bandgap of bulk MoS2 (Eg = 1.2 eV) and monolayer MoS2 (Eg = 1.8 eV) (Supplementary file).

Fig. 6 (a) shows the device's schematic consisting of N-CQDs/MoS2 as active heterojunction, ITO coated glass substrate and Ag served as bottom and top contacts, respectively. Both top and bottom contacts N-CQDs/Ag and MoS2/ITO are almost ohmic [45]. So, the current rectification characteristics mainly originated from the N-CQDs/MoS2 interface. Fig. 6(b) depicts the dark current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure. The current rectification in the positive voltage region reveals that there must be a p-n junction or Schottky junction at the N-CQDs/MoS2 interface. While I–V characteristics of bare MoS2 fabricated over ITO substrate show nearly linear behavior with a slight rectification (Inset 6(b)). The dark I–V characteristics have been replotted on the semilogarithmic scale to calculate the ideality factor (n) and current rectification ratio as shown in Fig. 6(c). Ideality factor was calculated for three different regions of the curve and it was found to be 1.27 for initial voltage region (V < 0.1 V), 6.25 for middle voltage region (0.1 V < V < 0.36 V) and 13.49 for higher voltages (V > 0.36 V). The ideality factor for lower voltage regions (V < 0.36 V) is due to the band-to-band recombination in the junction region. While for higher voltages (V > 0.36 V), the calculated ideality factor reflects the series resistance of the quantum dimensional heterostructure due to the carrier trapping in lattice defects formed by lattice mismatch, impurities and some voids left in the heterostructure. Current rectification calculated from the dark ln I–V characteristic was found to be 10 at ±1.5 V. The comparison of I–V characteristics in the dark and under AM 1.5 G solar illuminations with intensity 100 mW/cm2 is shown in Fig. 6(d). It clearly demonstrates a significant shift in the I–V curve on solar radiations illuminations. The solar I–V characteristics have been replotted in the scale of short-circuit current (Isc) and open-circuit voltage (Voc) to calculate solar cell parameters. Fig. 6(e) shows the replot of the I–V curve under solar illumination in voltage ranges 0 to Voc. The recorded value of Isc and Voc for the 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure are 13.49 mA and 0.83V, respectively. The fill factor of the quantum dimensional heterostructure based photovoltaic device was calculated using the relation:
F F =
P m a x
V o c
× I s c
, where P
max is the maximum power delivered by the heterostructure at particular value of current and voltage i.e. I
m and V
m, respectively. The power conversion efficiency of the heterostructure was calculated using the relation:
η =
F F
× I s c
× V o c
E
×
A
, where E represents input power, A is the area of heterojunction and the product
F F × I s c
× V o c
represents maximum power converted by the device for a particular value of current and voltage. The calculated value of the fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (η) of the fabricated 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure device are 36.26% and 4.06%, respectively. The reason behind the lower value of the fill factor must be the larger value of the ideality factor, as the fill factor mainly depends on the series resistance of the junction (Rs) and ideality factor (n). Fill factor decreases with an increase in both ideality factor and series resistance [46]. The recorded significantly high power conversion efficiency is due to the considerably enormous open-circuit voltage. The higher value of Voc is factually relaying compatible band alignment energy levels of MoS2 and N-CQDs in heterojunction formation. The schematic energy band diagram is plotted to understand the alignment of energy bands in N-CQDs/MoS2 quantum dimensional heterostructure and carrier transport mechanism under the illumination of solar radiations (Fig. 7 (b)). The reported values of electron affinities of N-CQDs and MoS2 are 3.25 eV and 4.22 eV, respectively, which are represented by χ N-CQDs, and χ MoS2 [28,47]. To achieve the energy equilibrium, the alignment of fermi levels of both materials occurs via electrons flow towards the MoS2 layer from the N-CQDs. The conduction band (CB) of MoS2 lies lower than the LUMO of N-CQDs, while the valence band (VB) of MoS2 lies slightly higher than the HOMO of N-CQDs [16]. This energy difference between the LUMO of N-CQDs and CB of MoS2 acts as an energy barrier for electrons of the MoS2 and prevents them to further drift towards N-CQDs. Thus, a depletion-like region is formed at the interface of the fabricated heterostructure. The N-CQDs mimic a p-type semiconductor in the equilibrium condition, whereas MoS2 intrinsically shows the n-type semiconducting nature. Therefore, 0D-2D quantum dimensional heterostructure acts as a semiconducting p-n junction. The electron-hole pairs are generated on both sides of the heterostructure under the illumination of solar radiations. The electrons in HOMO energy levels of N-CQDs get excited and move to the LUMO energy level of N-CQDs in the UV region of the solar spectrum. On the other hand, the MoS2 side of the junction responds to the visible and NIR region of the solar spectrum. The depletion-like region is formed due to barrier height prevents the recombination of electron-hole pairs and provides the built-in potential to move electrons to the MoS2 side while holes to the N-CQDs side are further collected at the metallic electrodes. The N-CQDs layer serves as the hole transport layer as well as the electron blocking layer for diminishing the recombination of carriers at the metal electrode interface. It leads to a lower saturation current density, which contributes to a higher Voc of the quantum dimensional heterostructure device. Also, the higher barrier height must be the major responsible factor for the enormously high open-circuit voltage.

The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra of the N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure were recorded for wavelength ranges 350 nm–800 nm (Fig. 7(b)). The quantum dimensional heterostructure-based photovoltaic device exhibits an excellent photoresponse in the recorded wavelength ranges. Higher external quantum efficiency in the lower wavelength ranges supports the multiple exciton generation phenomena in the N-CQDs. For wavelength between 450 nm to 700 nm, the prominent EQE enhancement can be attributed to the re-emitted photons by the N-CQDs layer at the N-CQDs/MoS2 interface, and absorption in the MoS2 layer generate more electrons-hole pairs. EIS spectra of N-CQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure was recorded under AM 1.5G 100 mW/cm2 solar illumination with applied forward bias voltage equal to its open-circuit voltage (Voc). Here, the EIS spectra was studied using a three-electrode assembly in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. The Ag/AgCl, Pt, and fabricated heterostructure devices were used as reference electrode, counter electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The spectra were scanned in a frequency range of 0.1 MHz–1 mHz with 10 mV AC perturbation voltage. The solar radiations generate electro-hole pairs near the junction interface in the quantum dimensional heterostructure after passing through the upper N-CQDs layer. Electrolyte generates a current due to applied voltage and electrons move towards the MoS2 layer. EIS Curves (Nyquist plot) for the quantum dimensional heterostructure is shown in Fig. 8 . The Nyquist plot of the heterostructure shows a semicircle nature with a small radius which indicates a fast carrier transportation occurs at the junction interface.

Table 1 illustrates the photovoltaic parameters of various heterojunction based solar cells containing MoS2 and CQDs reported in literature. The current N-CQDs/MoS2 based device fabricated in the present work shows excellent PCE compared to other quantum dimensional heterostructures. Therefore, this work provides a breakthrough in the direction of the quantum dimensional (0D/2D) heterojunction-based, cost-effective, and efficient photovoltaic devices. A novel 0D N-CQDs possess strong quantum confinement and quantum size effect, and 2D MoS2 have better absorption and layer-dependent energy bandgap.

In summary, N-CQDs/MoS2, a 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure was fabricated on ITO coated glass substrate for solar photovoltaic application. The MoS2 layer was fabricated using DC magnetron sputtering, while N-CQDs were deposited on a MoS2/ITO coated glass substrate via electrophoretic deposition technique. The crystalline phase, surface texture, morphology, and bonding characteristics of the heterostructure layers were confirmed by X-ray diffraction pattern, AFM, FE-SEM, TEM images, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The fabricated heterostructure provides excellent solar photovoltaic characteristics. The calculated value of Isc, Voc, FF, and power conversion efficiency were 13.49 mA, 0.83 V, 36.26%, and 4.06%, respectively. Recorded external quantum efficiency spectrum supports the solar photovoltaic performance of the device. These results clearly show N-CQDs/MoS2 based 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure provides excellent opportunities for higher efficient photovoltaic devices with offering enormously high open-circuit voltage. This work is a step ahead of the new era of non-toxic 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure-based photovoltaic devices.
All research data that support the findings of this study are included within the article (and in supplementary file).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Krishan Kumar gratefully thanks to University Grant Commission (UGC), India for awarding a Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).
